instruction: Extract all core computer science concepts from the following lecture JSON. Return only a newline-separated list of unique concepts. Do not explain anything.

text_blocks.text: You Hear a Voice Whisper: “The Memory Layout is a Lie”

runs.text: You Hear a Voice Whisper: “The Memory Layout is a Lie”

text_blocks.text: Today, we take a deeper dive at how dynamic memory is allocated in the heap.

runs.text: Today, we take a deeper dive at how dynamic memory is allocated in the heap.

text_blocks.text: We have looked at malloc and calloc.
They stake out space in the heap and return an address.

runs.text: They stake out space in the heap and return an address.

text_blocks.text: Right now, we live in a nice ideal world.

runs.text: Right now, we live in a nice ideal world.

text_blocks.text: We have access to all of the memory.

runs.text: We have access to all of the memory.

text_blocks.text: The OS is lying to our program.

runs.text: The OS is lying to our program.

text_blocks.text: We will investigate this lie later in the course.

runs.text: We will investigate this lie later in the course.

text_blocks.text: It is a puzzle without any optimal solution. Welcome to computers!

runs.text: It is a puzzle without any optimal solution. Welcome to computers!

text_blocks.text: Stack access often does not deviate much.

runs.text: Stack access often does not deviate much.

text_blocks.text: We allocate a little bit at a time.

runs.text: We allocate a little bit at a time.

text_blocks.text: We allocate and free the memory VERY often.

runs.text: We allocate and free the memory VERY often.

text_blocks.text: Heap allocations have many access patterns that are possible.

runs.text: Heap allocations have many access patterns that are possible.

text_blocks.text: You might allocate a lot at a time and keep it around for a long time. Or a short time.

runs.text: You might allocate a lot at a time and keep it around for a long time. Or a short time.

text_blocks.text: You might allocate a lot of small things, instead.

runs.text: You might allocate a lot of small things, instead.

text_blocks.text: Maybe you do a little bit of everything?

runs.text: Maybe you do a little bit of everything?

text_blocks.text: Often, such patterns are not easy to predict.

runs.text: Often, such patterns are not easy to predict.

text_blocks.text: Do you get a big file as input? A small file?

runs.text: Do you get a big file as input? A small file?

runs.text: Allocations could happen in a nice order.

runs.text: When something is allocated, it can be allocated after everything else.

runs.text: When freed, it makes room for new things.

text_blocks.text: Moving around something in the heap is hard!

runs.text: Moving around something in the heap is hard!

text_blocks.text: Any pointers referring to data within a block must be updated.

runs.text: Any pointers referring to data within a block must be updated.

runs.text: Finding these pointers automatically is effectively as difficult as garbage collection.

text_blocks.text: Ensuring continuous space is difficult when you do not know how much you will ultimately need.

runs.text: Ensuring continuous space is difficult when you do not know how much you will ultimately need.

text_blocks.text: This is exactly why linked lists exist!
Since a linked list allocates on every append.

text_blocks.text: Segments are the “code”, “data”, “heap” separation. You fault by doing something the segment does not allow. (write to read-only memory)
